1: Isaiah 2 opens with a second superscription ('the word that Isaiah son of Amoz saw') — suggesting this may have originally been an independent oracle collection, or that the chapter introduces a new section. The verb 'saw' (chazah) is the visionary term for prophetic reception (cf. the superscription of Isaiah 1:1: 'the vision that Isaiah saw'). The oracle that follows (Isaiah 2:2-4) is among the most famous in the entire OT and is found in nearly identical form in Micah 4:1-3, raising the question of which prophet quoted which (or whether both drew on a common temple liturgy).
2: 'In the last days, the mountain where the LORD's house stands will be established as the highest of all mountains' — 'in the last days' (be'acharit hayyamim, in the end of the days) is the eschatological formula that introduces the vision's temporal frame: this is not a prediction for the immediate future but a picture of the world's ultimate destination. Zion as the highest mountain: in Canaanite mythology, Baal's divine mountain was Zaphon (the far north), the meeting place of the gods. Isaiah's oracle relocates the cosmic mountain to Zion (YHWH's actual dwelling) and declares it will be exalted above all. 'All nations will stream toward it' (venaharu elav kol-hagoyim) — the universal pilgrimage that reverses Babel's scattering.
3: 'Many peoples will come and say: "Come, let us go up to the mountain of the LORD, to the house of the God of Jacob, that he may teach us his ways"' — the nations voluntarily making pilgrimage to Zion to receive instruction (torah) from YHWH is a reversal of the Deuteronomic pattern: normally Israel receives Torah at Sinai and the nations do not. Here the nations come to Jerusalem to receive YHWH's teaching — making Zion the new Sinai from which divine instruction radiates universally. 'For the teaching will go out from Zion and the word of the LORD from Jerusalem' — the torah-word pairing makes explicit: Zion = eschatological Sinai. Revelation 21:24-26 (the nations bringing their glory into the New Jerusalem) reflects this vision.
4: 'He will settle disputes between nations and arbitrate for many peoples. Then they will beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks' — YHWH as international arbiter (shaphat, judge) and arbitrator (yakiach, adjudicate/correct) makes war unnecessary: if YHWH settles disputes, the nations need no weapons. The specific agricultural conversions: cherev (swords) into ittim (plowshares — iron blades for breaking ground); chanit (spears/lances) into mazmerat (pruning hooks for vineyards). The conversion from military to agricultural implements symbolizes the transformation of energy from killing to growing. Joel 3:10 deliberately reverses this oracle ('beat your plowshares into swords') for the judgment-day context — assuming Isaiah's vision as its point of departure.
5: 'Come, house of Jacob, let us walk in the light of the LORD!' — the transitional call that moves from the eschatological vision (Isaiah 2:2-4) to the present indictment (Isaiah 2:6-22). The logical inference: since YHWH's light will one day illuminate all nations, Israel should walk in that light now. The 'light of the LORD' (be'or Yhwh) connects to the later Isaianic imagery of the Servant as 'a light for the nations' (Isaiah 42:6; Isaiah 49:6) and to Isaiah 60:1-3 ('Arise, shine, for your light has come... the nations will come to your light'). The call 'Come' (leku) is the same invitation extended to the nations in Isaiah 2:3 — inviting Israel to join the eschatological pilgrimage proactively.
6: 'For you, LORD, have rejected your people, the house of Jacob, because they have adopted practices from the east and practice divination like the Philistines, and they make agreements with foreigners' — the specific covenant violations: eastward practices (qedem, Mesopotamian divination, astrology, and magic), Philistine divination (qosmim kafelishtim, same as Philistines who consulted Baal-Zebub, 2 Kings 1:2-3), and 'agreements with foreigners' (yashpequ beyalde nocharim, making covenants with the sons of foreigners — political alliances that substituted foreign military power for YHWH-trust). Ahaz's alliance with Assyria against Syria/Israel (2 Kings 16:7-8) is the paradigm case Isaiah opposed.
7: 'Their land is full of silver and gold with no end to their treasures; their land is full of horses with no end to their chariots' — the triple 'full' (male'ah) indictment: silver/gold, horses/chariots, idols (Isaiah 2:8). These are precisely the three things Deuteronomy 17:16-17 prohibits the king from multiplying: 'He must not acquire great numbers of horses for himself... He must not accumulate large amounts of silver and gold.' The military equipment (horses, chariots) = dependence on Egyptian military technology (Isaiah 31:1-3 develops this: woe to those who go down to Egypt for horses). Wealth accumulation and military power are the alternatives to YHWH-trust.
8: 'Their land is full of idols; they worship what their own hands have made, what their own fingers have fashioned' — the idol polemic: the gods of the nations are human artifacts (made by hands and fashioned by fingers). Isaiah develops the most sustained and devastating idol polemic in the OT (Isaiah 44:9-20: the craftsman who uses half the tree for firewood and half to make a god). The satire rests on the premise that a divine being who depends on human craftsmanship for its existence cannot rescue its creators (Isaiah 46:1-2: Bel and Nebo, Babylonian gods, must be carried as cargo, unable to carry their people).
9: 'Mankind is brought low and every person humbled — do not forgive them!' — the anticipation of the Day of the LORD judgment (which Isaiah 2:10-21 describe): both the common person (adam, generic humanity) and the great person (ish, the individual of significance) will be humbled. 'Do not forgive them!' (al-tissa lahem, do not lift/pardon for them) is a prophetic intercessory-renunciation formula — Isaiah is told not to intercede for judgment's reversal (cf. Jeremiah 7:16: 'Do not pray for this people or offer any plea or petition for them'). The refusal to intercede marks a point of no return in the covenant relationship.
10: 'Hide yourself in the rocky cliffs and bury yourself in the dust from before the terrifying presence of the LORD and the splendor of his majesty' — the call to hiding before the theophanic approach of YHWH. The rocky cliffs (tsur, bedrock) and dust (afar) as hiding places evoke the cave in which Elijah hid during the Horeb theophany (1 Kings 19:9-13). The 'terrifying presence' (pachad Yhwh) and 'splendor of his majesty' (hadar ge'ono) are the physical manifestations of the holy warrior YHWH approaching in judgment. Revelation 6:15-16 echoes this verse: 'They called to the mountains and the rocks, "Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who sits on the throne."'
11: 'The arrogant gaze of mankind will be brought low and human pride will be humbled; on that day the LORD alone will be exalted' — 'the arrogant gaze' (einei gavhut ha'adam, the haughty eyes of man) is the specific physiognomy of pride: the lifted head and elevated gaze of the person who considers himself above others. The Proverbs list 'haughty eyes' as the first of the seven things YHWH hates (Proverbs 6:17). The Day-of-the-LORD formula appears here for the first time in the chapter: 'on that day' (bayyom hahu) the polarity reverses — human elevation becomes humiliation; divine exaltation is absolute. This is the eschatological reversal of the Magnificat (Luke 1:51-52: 'He has scattered those who are proud in the inmost thoughts of their hearts').
12: 'For the LORD of hosts has appointed a day against everything proud and exalted, against everything lifted high — it will be humbled' — the 'day of the LORD of hosts' (yom le-Yhwh tseva'ot) introduces the comprehensive list of targets in Isaiah 2:12-16. The divine warrior YHWH has set his appointment (yom, day; mo'ed, appointed time) against every manifestation of created greatness. 'Everything proud and exalted' (kol-ge'eh verum) and 'everything lifted high' (kol-nissa) — the Hebrew uses three synonyms for elevation/pride to indicate the totality of YHWH's judgment against human and natural pretension to greatness.
13: 'Against all the towering cedars of Lebanon and all the oaks of Bashan' — the first specific targets: Lebanon's cedars (erez halevavon) and Bashan's oaks (allone habashan). The cedars of Lebanon were the largest and most impressive trees in the ancient Near Eastern world — used for temple construction (1 Kings 5:6-10), royal palaces (1 Kings 7:2), and as a symbol of might and pride (Psalms 92:12: 'the righteous flourish like a palm tree and grow like a cedar in Lebanon'). Bashan's oaks were famous for their strength (Ezekiel 27:6; Amos 2:9). Natural greatness — the tallest, strongest trees — falls under the Day of the LORD.
14: 'Against all the high mountains and against all the elevated hills' — the geological landscape of pride: high mountains (harim haramim, the lifted-up mountains) and elevated hills (hageva'ot hanissa'ot, the exalted hills). In the ancient cosmology, mountains were sacred high places where the divine council met (Isaiah 14:13: 'I will ascend to the heavens... I will sit enthroned on the mount of assembly'; the Baal mythology of Ugarit set the divine council on Mount Zaphon). The elevated terrain that was associated with divine power will itself be humbled — no natural high place survives YHWH's universal Day.
15: 'Against every tall tower and against every fortified wall' — the human architectural achievements: tall towers (kol-migdal gavoah, every tower of height) and fortified walls (kol-chomah beturah, every defended/cut wall). Towers and walls were the technology of power in the ancient world — the Assyrian siege-ramp system was specifically designed to overcome such structures (2 Kings 19:32). The tower of Babel (Genesis 11:4: 'a tower that reaches to the heavens') is the paradigm of human architectural pride meeting divine opposition. Military fortifications built as alternatives to YHWH-trust (cf. Isaiah 22:8-11: 'You built a reservoir between the two walls... but you did not look to the One who made it') fall on the Day of the LORD.
16: 'Against all the merchant ships of Tarshish and against all their prized vessels' — 'ships of Tarshish' (aniyyot tarshish) were the large, ocean-going merchant vessels (Tarshish = possibly Tartessus in Spain, the far western limit of the ancient Mediterranean world). Solomon built ships of Tarshish for the Ophir trade (1 Kings 9:26-28; Isaiah 10:22). The Phoenician-Israelite maritime commerce represented the height of economic power and international reach. 'Prized vessels' (kol-sekiyyot hachemdah, all the delightful/beautiful crafts) — the luxury goods of Mediterranean trade. Commercial power falls alongside military power on the universal Day.
17: 'Human pride will be brought low and human arrogance will be humbled; on that day the LORD alone will be exalted' — the exact repetition of Isaiah 2:11 forms an inclusio for the Day-of-the-LORD list (Isaiah 2:12-16). The repetition is deliberate liturgical reinforcement: before and after the comprehensive catalog of pride-objects (trees, mountains, towers, ships), the theological point is restated: the Day will simultaneously humble all human and natural greatness while exalting YHWH alone. The exclusive exaltation of YHWH on that day is the positive eschatological goal; the humbling of everything else is its necessary corollary.
18: 'And the idols will completely disappear' — brief but total: the idols (ha'eliylim, the nonentities/worthless ones — a play on elohim, gods, by adding the diminutive suggesting their nothingness) will 'completely disappear' (chalilah ya'avoru, will pass away entirely). The Day of the LORD that humbles the great trees and mountains also eliminates the idols that were worshiped under them (Isaiah 2:8) and in their shade (cf. Isaiah 1:29). The idol polemic connects: what human hands make (Isaiah 2:8) disappears on YHWH's Day.
19: 'People will go into the caverns of the rocks and the holes of the ground to escape the terrifying presence of the LORD and the splendor of his majesty, when he rises to make the earth tremble' — almost identical to Isaiah 2:10, the panic-flight to caves is now narrated (rather than commanded). 'When he rises to make the earth tremble' (bekumoh la'arats lehachrid) — the theophanic approach of YHWH causes seismic disturbance (the connection between divine approach and earthquake is in Psalms 18:7; Isaiah 68:8; Habakkuk 3:6). The phrase will reappear in Amos 8:8 and Nahum 1:5. Revelation 6:15-17 draws directly on this verse for the sixth-seal earthquake.
20: 'On that day people will throw away their silver idols and gold idols, which they made for themselves to worship, flinging them to the moles and the bats' — the dramatic abandonment of the idols: thrown to the moles (chafar perot, digging moles or possibly prairie mice) and bats (atalef, the bat) — creatures of dark, underground, and hidden places. The precious metals wasted on idol-making (silver and gold from Isaiah 2:7) are now flung to vermin. The irony is acute: the craftsmen who made the idols from precious materials (Isaiah 2:8) now throw them away in panic. The idols provide no refuge when YHWH appears — only his mountain-caves protect (Isaiah 2:19, sarcastically).
21: 'They will hide in the clefts of the rocky crags and the fissures of the cliffs to escape the terrifying presence of the LORD and the splendor of his majesty, when he rises to make the earth tremble' — the third repetition of the hiding-from-YHWH motif (Isaiah 2:10, 19, 21) creates a litany of panic. The threefold repetition is a rhetorical device of intensification (the same structure as the Day-of-the-LORD catalog in Isaiah 2:12-17). The rocky crags and cliffs form a grotesque shelter: the same rocks that the worshipers fled to from YHWH's face (Exodus 33:22: YHWH sheltered Moses in the cleft of a rock to show his glory) now become hiding places from that same glory.
22: 'Stop trusting in mere mortals, who have nothing but a breath in their nostrils — what are they really worth?' — the chapter's conclusion draws the practical inference from the Day-of-the-LORD vision: if all human greatness will be humbled (trees, mountains, towers, ships, idols), trust in human power is foolish. 'Breath in their nostrils' (neshamah be'appo, breath in his nose) echoes Genesis 2:7 (YHWH breathed into man's nostrils the breath of life) — the fragility of human life is its breath-dependence. 'What are they really worth?' (bameh necheshav hu, in what is he to be accounted?) — the final rhetorical question diminishes all human pretension to zero.
